text:
Imagine this: you're about to embark on an exciting journey to one of the most captivating cities in the world—Copenhagen (哥本哈根) ! Now, you are preparing for this trip.

You need to list 6 interesting spots in Copenhagen as the potential destinations, as well as the reason for choosing them, the admission prices, and the opening days, and list 6 important “travel tips” about Copenhagen.

To better evaluate the result, please organize your answers according to the following format:

Example:
	Location 1:
	location: UCLA
	reason: it's a famous university
	price: free
	opening days: all days
Location 2:
	...
Tips:
	1. Parking fee is high
	...
Amarienborg
It's the home of the Danish royal family. You can witness the change of guard at 12:00, which is a unique experience.
$1.25 (reservation required)
Open all week, the change of guard happens at 12:00 daily.
Frederiks Kirke
It's a famous modern architecture. The church's design and architecture are worth exploring.
Open from Tuesday to Friday, 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM.
The Little Mermaid Statue
It's the character from Grimm's Fairy Tales. The statue is an iconic symbol of Copenhagen.
Free
Open all week, 24 hours.
Noma
It's a world-famous restaurant offering a culinary masterpiece. A dinner here would be a memorable experience.
Open all week, dinner service starts at 7:00 PM.
Rundetaarn
It's an astronomy tower of the 17th century, offering a panoramic view of Copenhagen.
Open all week, 10:00 AM - 6:00 PM.
Christiansborg Palace
It's one of the most significant landmarks in Copenhagen, both historically and politically.
Free
Open from Tuesday to Sunday, 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM.
The type of power sockets used in Denmark are Type A and Type K. Don't forget to bring an adapter if necessary.
The official currency in Denmark is the Danish Krone (DKK). Most places do not accept euros.
There are in total 6 metros in Copenhagen. Visitors can reach anywhere by metro. It's the most convenient way to travel around the city.
The weather in Copenhagen in December is below -15 degrees Celsius. Make sure to bring warm clothes.
The official language is Danish, but English is widely spoken in Copenhagen. However, learning a few Danish phrases like "hej" (hello), "farvel" (thank you), and "tak" (goodbye) might be helpful.
Copenhagen follows left-hand drive. Be careful if you plan to rent a car or bike.
